original_reasoning: Arabic sabil/dabil = calamity, disaster (also a tribal usage variant). Greek eisbolai = nom.pl of eisbole 'inroads, invasions, irruptions' (eis+ballo 'throw into'). Calamities and invasions: invasion IS the disaster. Strong direct link.
Arabic names or designates; Greek means signs or marks, a plausible signification chain.

Arabic al-fah = wide-mouthed, eloquent (from fam/fah 'mouth'). Greek Polyphemos = 'much-spoken/much-famed' (poly+pheme 'speech'), also the Cyclops's name. Both encode mouth/speech prominence — wide-mouth and much-spoken. The conceptual chain is direct via the mouth-speech mafhum. Strong link.
الأحمق and nonsense/idle talk share a clear folly/stupidity field, though one is a person and the other speech.
Greek suffix marks speaking in the language of a people or place; Arabic gloss includes Asyut, so both touch the place-to-language side of meaning.
Arabic sabkha often foregrounds pale salt-whiteness, and Greek λευκίσκος belongs directly to the white/whitish field.
Arabic includes growth and emergence of blossom after enclosure, which can plausibly meet Greek becoming through a coming-forth core.
Arabic marks something foreign or non-Arabic, giving a plausible chain to speaking in the language of the Indians.
